worth_it_explanation: Assets are free to add and they enlarge the ad, give people more ways to click, and feed Ad Strength, so thin asset coverage holds back both the click-through rate and the auction position the account could earn at the same bid. The check is quick. The assets table in Google Ads shows what is attached and at which level, and the gaps read at a glance. The fix is configuration, not a rebuild, and an account-level set of sitelinks, callouts, structured snippets, and images covers most of the gap on the day it is saved.
intro: Assets are the extra pieces Google Ads can show with an ad, such as sitelinks, callouts, structured snippets, images, and the business name and logo. They enlarge the ad, add links and detail, and count toward Ad Strength, so the eligible inventory shapes how often an ad shows in an expanded form and how it competes in the auction. These checks confirm that the account has the asset types its campaigns can use, that assets sit at the level where they stay relevant, and that the content is current rather than stale.

audit_checks.title: Search campaigns run with no sitelink assets or fewer than the number needed to show
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Sitelinks add links to specific pages and enlarge the ad, and they feed Ad Strength. A campaign with none, or with fewer than the minimum Google needs to display them, forfeits that extra real estate and the clicks it would draw at the same bid.

educate.base:
Sitelink assets are extra links shown beneath a Search ad that send people to specific pages, for example a pricing page, a store-locator page, or a contact page. They give the ad more ways to earn a click, they enlarge its footprint on the results page, and Google counts them toward Ad Strength, the rating that reflects how complete an ad's assets are.

Sitelinks do not always show. Google needs a minimum number eligible before it displays any, and it decides at auction time whether showing them improves the result. A campaign with one sitelink, or none, has nothing for Google to display, so the ad runs in its plain form and competes for the same click with less surface area than a rival ad that carries a full set.

Sitelinks can sit at the account, campaign, or ad-group level. An account-level set applies everywhere unless a campaign or ad group has its own, which gives broad coverage from a single place. The audit reads the assets table for each Search campaign to see how many eligible sitelinks it has and whether any are disapproved. A disapproved sitelink does not count toward the eligible total.

A few signs point to a sitelink gap worth closing:
  • A Search campaign shows no sitelink assets at any level
  • The campaign has only one sitelink, below the count Google needs to display them
  • Sitelinks exist but several are disapproved, leaving too few eligible
You may want to consider building at least four sitelinks with descriptions at the account level, then letting individual campaigns override them where a more specific link fits.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, sitelinks map well to the category and utility pages shoppers look for, such as a sale page, a shipping-and-returns page, a size-guide page, and a store-locator page. You may want to confirm the linked pages still exist and resolve. A sitelink to a retired category page sends people to a dead end and can be disapproved.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, sitelinks suit the pages that move someone toward an inquiry, such as a pricing page, a case-studies page, a contact page, and a page that explains the service. You may want to point at least one sitelink at the page where the form lives, so the asset shortens the path to the conversion rather than adding a detour.

investigate.intro: Confirm, per Search campaign, how many eligible sitelink assets it has across the account, campaign, and ad-group levels, and whether any are disapproved.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Assets', and select 'Sitelinks' as the asset type.
Set the table to show the association level and status so you can see which sitelinks apply to each Search campaign and whether each is approved.

For each Search campaign, count the eligible (approved) sitelinks that apply to it, including any inherited from the account level. Note any campaign with none, with only one, or with several disapproved.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Search campaign, the number of eligible sitelink assets that apply to it and the number disapproved. A campaign with at least the minimum Google needs to display sitelinks, all approved, passes. A campaign with none, with only one, or with too few eligible after disapprovals is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the eligible count.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the eligible sitelink count for each Search campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Search campaigns on {{site_name}} run with no sitelink assets, or with fewer than Google needs to display them. Sitelinks add links to specific pages, enlarge the ad, and feed Ad Strength, so a campaign without them competes for the same click with less surface area than a fuller competing ad. {{voice_recommend}} building at least four sitelinks with descriptions at the account level so every campaign inherits them, then adding campaign-specific sitelinks where a more relevant page fits. After the change, confirm in the assets table that each Search campaign now carries enough approved sitelinks to be eligible to show.

audit_checks.title: Search campaigns serve text-only with no image assets attached
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Image assets let a Search ad show a relevant picture beside the text on eligible placements, which enlarges the ad and can raise the click-through rate. A campaign with none runs text-only and forfeits that placement, and the missing image also lowers Ad Strength.

educate.base:
Image assets attach pictures to a Search campaign so an eligible ad can show a relevant image beside its text on the search results page. The image does not replace the text ad. It enlarges the ad's footprint on placements where Google decides an image improves the result, and it counts toward Ad Strength.

A Search campaign with no image assets can only ever run as a text ad. On any placement where an image-enabled competitor shows a picture, the text-only ad takes less space and gives the searcher less to react to. The campaign also carries a lower Ad Strength than it would with images attached. Image assets are part of what that rating measures.

Image assets follow Google's specifications for aspect ratios, file size, and content. A square (1:1) and a landscape (1.91:1) image give Google both shapes it can place, and the picture should show the product or service rather than text on the image, which Google restricts. Like other assets, images can sit at the account, campaign, or ad-group level, so an account-level set provides a floor that every campaign inherits.

A few signs point to an image-asset gap:
  • A Search campaign shows no image assets at any level
  • Only one aspect ratio is present, so Google has fewer shapes to place
  • The images on file are dated or no longer represent the offer
You may want to consider adding both a square and a landscape image at the account level first, then attaching campaign-specific images where a campaign promotes a distinct product or service.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, image assets pair naturally with the product or category a campaign promotes, so a campaign for a specific line can show that product beside the text. You may want to confirm the image reflects the current catalog. A picture of a discontinued product undercuts the click it earns.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, image assets work best when they show the service or its outcome rather than a generic stock photo. The picture sets the expectation for the page behind the click. You may want to choose an image that matches the landing page so the ad and the page read as one offer.

investigate.intro: Confirm, per Search campaign, whether image assets are attached and whether more than one aspect ratio is present.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Assets', and select 'Images' as the asset type.
Set the table to show the association level and status so you can see which images apply to each Search campaign and whether each is approved.

For each Search campaign, note whether any image assets apply to it and whether both a square and a landscape image are present. Record any campaign with none, and any with a single aspect ratio only.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Search campaign, whether image assets apply to it and which aspect ratios are present. A campaign with at least one approved image, and ideally both a square and a landscape ratio, passes. A campaign with no image assets is a fail recorded with the campaign name. A campaign with a single aspect ratio is recorded as a partial gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether image assets apply to each Search campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Search campaigns on {{site_name}} have no image assets attached, so those campaigns serve text-only. An image asset lets the ad show a relevant picture beside the text on eligible placements, which enlarges the ad and feeds Ad Strength, and a text-only campaign forfeits both. {{voice_recommend}} adding at least one square and one landscape image at the account level so every campaign inherits a set, then attaching campaign-specific images where a campaign promotes a distinct product or service. After the change, confirm in the assets table that approved images apply to each Search campaign.

audit_checks.title: Assets live only at the account level where campaign-specific assets would be more relevant
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Account-level assets apply everywhere, which gives broad coverage but generic content. A campaign that sells one product still inherits sitelinks and callouts written for the whole business, so the ad shows less relevant extensions than a campaign-level set would, costing some clicks and relevance.

educate.base:
Assets can be attached at three levels in Google Ads. An account-level asset applies to every eligible campaign, a campaign-level asset applies to one campaign, and an ad-group-level asset applies to one ad group. A more specific level overrides a broader one, so a campaign that has its own sitelinks shows those instead of the account-level set.

Account-level assets are the right floor. They give every campaign coverage from one place and prevent the empty-asset case. The trade-off is relevance. A single set of sitelinks, callouts, and structured snippets written for the whole business cannot speak to a campaign that promotes one product line or one service. The asset still shows, but it points at general pages and makes general claims rather than the specific ones the campaign could make.

The audit looks for campaigns whose theme is narrower than the account-level assets they inherit. A campaign built around a single product, a single location, or a single service is a candidate for its own sitelinks and callouts that name that thing, link to its page, and state its specific benefit. The goal is not to replace the account-level floor everywhere. It is to add a campaign-level set where the extra specificity earns more relevant clicks.

A few signs suggest a campaign would benefit from its own assets:
  • A tightly themed campaign inherits only account-level sitelinks that link to general pages
  • The account-level callouts make whole-business claims that do not fit the campaign's offer
  • A location or product campaign shows structured snippets that list everything rather than its own range
You may want to keep the account-level assets as the default and add campaign-level assets only where a campaign's theme is specific enough that tailored extensions would read as more relevant.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a campaign that promotes one category inherits sitelinks aimed at the whole catalog unless it has its own. You may want to give such a campaign sitelinks that link to subpages within its category, so the extensions match what the campaign is actually selling.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a campaign for one service inherits callouts that describe the full practice unless it has its own. You may want to add campaign-level callouts that name the specific service's benefits, so the ad's claims match the page the click leads to.

investigate.intro: Identify tightly themed campaigns that inherit only account-level assets and would carry more relevant extensions with their own campaign-level set.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Assets', and review each asset type with the table set to show the association level.
Note which assets apply only at the account level and which campaigns rely entirely on that inherited set.

For each tightly themed campaign, for example one built around a single product, location, or service, judge whether the inherited assets speak to that theme or only to the business as a whole. Record the campaigns where a campaign-level set would be more relevant.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists the campaigns whose theme is narrower than the account-level assets they inherit and notes, per campaign, whether it has any campaign-level assets of its own. A tightly themed campaign with its own relevant sitelinks and callouts passes. A tightly themed campaign relying only on generic account-level assets is recorded as an opportunity with the campaign name and the asset types that would benefit from a campaign-level version.
evaluate.fallback_message: List the tightly themed campaigns and their asset levels above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several tightly themed campaigns on {{site_name}} rely only on account-level assets, so a campaign that sells one product or service shows sitelinks and callouts written for the whole business. The extensions still serve, yet they point at general pages and make general claims rather than the specific ones the campaign could make, which costs relevance and some clicks. {{voice_recommend}} keeping the account-level assets as the default and adding campaign-level sitelinks and callouts to the narrowly themed campaigns, so each one shows extensions that name its offer and link to its page. After the change, confirm in the assets table that the campaign-level assets apply to the intended campaigns.
